author: @pecilijevelicanstveni4973
text:
Why do this? Why produce electricity and send it to the battery when it is simpler to transfer the power to the generator and produce electricity.
There is simple solution.
Horizontal shaft: on one end is turbine and on the other is one bevel gear. On the vertical generator rotor shaft there is another bevel gear. The angle between shafts is 90 degrees. Whenever turbine rotate around the vertical axis the bevel gear on its shaft is rotating around the generator bevel gear and is coupled with it (like planetary gear) and transfers the power. In one direction the rotation will be a little faster and in the opposite direction a little slower, which has no effect on the production of electricity. In that case, the only moving mass is the turbine and the generator can even be placed at the base of the column! The only connection to the turbine is a long vertical shaft.
The generator stator is of course stationary so the wires from him are stationary too.

author: @AETERNACOMMAND
text:
Hey Robert and community,
I develop a handbook on how to integrate automatic systems into nature, so that every human can live free and in best health from the fruits of this earth (paradise).

In my region (central europe) it is to cold to sleep outside without shelter. To counter this circumstance, a telluric current driven floor heating system would be great, and I think it would a perfect project for the channel.

I thought that a 50cm x 150cm closed rectangular water pipe, which is slightly angled in height at one pipe corner, in order to allow water flow through a water boiling resonator attached to the exterior pipe - would be a possible solution, which can be dug into the ground (soil).

It would be great if you or someone from the community would come up with such an automatic system which I (and everybody) can build efficiently from scavenged resources.

Best regards,
Quentin

author: @gregbloch80
text: The best solution I have seen yet is to let the wires hang down the middle of the tower and put a plug on them. Every few days you look at how many twists the wires have got and if necessary, unplug and untwist. With a 20' tower you can easily get a half dozen twists. This way you wires will last a lot longer before they are fatigued.

I've often wondered why they didn't use conductive rollers within rings onto an inner ring, rather like roller bearings, but with the outer in an insulated sleave/carrier, instead of slip rings/'brushes'. If you use tapered roller bearings in sprung carriers it deals with any wear issues.

author: @elvendragonhammer5433
text: When you mentioned slip rings I initially thought you meant actual slip rings, like 2 PCB's with the copper traces facing each other with conductive grease in between. Wires would be mounted on the through-hole side, like what's on real weathervane style wind turbines- <they can rotate in any direction to face the wind> or the multiple metal spindle w/ hollow pipe surrounding it style. (not sure what that one is called) Although both of those would work better as long term & under variable/harsher conditions I should have guessed you'd find the easiest & cheapest option. My only suggestion is if it's gonna be in a cold environment make sure the coating on the wire doesn't shrink & harden with the cold or it will break off @ the connection points or the coating will crack & short. I recommend silicone sleaved wire for that instead.

text: This is a really cool thing, but... I have yet to see any actually useful application of this. Any pointers? Like... you need something to be stationary, like a socket on a wall, pass it through a mechanism like this, and then run a motor with an axis.. or something. The axis will ruin everything. The only use I can see if a magnetically levitated disc with a light source on, that needs electricity...  Since the "axis" in this case will be light, we can pass through it. But you still need to levitate the disc, and you need some cleaver and not to complicated way of guiding the cables up and down every other rotation... Seems rather a lot? Am I wrong?

author: @totherarf
text:
Great idea ....... totally counter intuitive! 
Looking at the models it would seem the weak point is the flexing of the cables. Short term, no problem .... but longer term I think the copper will work harden and shear!

One thing that occurs to me is that you don't have to transmit power as electricity! In fact you could change things round a bit and have no problems at all!
ATM you have your magnets passing your coil to generate electricity. If you generated an alternating magnetic field and then used that field to cross a small air gap where your twisting action was you could then harvest the magnetic field generated in a coil mounted on the pole itself.
An alternative to that would be to transmit the rotating motion down vertically with a gear and pick that rotary motion (now on a vertical axis) to a shaft running along the shaft supporting the device. This may need a larger "tail" to stop the spinning movement arc generated!
A further "cop out" could be to have your turbine tethered to a spring so that when it stops blowing the spring will return the device to it's original position allowing for a simple flex running down the shaft to twist and untwist every time

As per usual these ideas are worth exactly what you paid for them ;0)

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Eventually, those wires are going to break.

The constant flexing causes work hardening and the copper will fatigue and fail.

Slip rings are, in fact, superior.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would have thought probably not in a wind turbine application - it might rotate maybe once in a day - if that - but an interesting mechanism non the less and it would be interesting to see exactly how many rotations a wind turbine actually goes through.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Even if you only go 180 degrees, over time, especially if you lack super fine, braided wire, you are going to see the natural work hardening properties of copper wire go into effect and the wire will fail.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: Yes, fatigue will be increasingly significant for rigid"er" conductors. A lot of smaller turbines just limit rotations (a very strong chain, or even a couple of cables lifting a heavy weight (a couple of slack turns, then it will self centre)  seeing as wind doesn't really come from all angles sequentially, they don't "wind up"  that often - large turbines are motor driven, simple to unwind every so often... (Esp when there is no wind, no stress, no loses.). Other arrangements of cabling / limiting elements, such as "clock spring" configurations can easily handle several rotations, dynamic self centring using a field function works...
